title: Elderberry and ginger decoction for immune support
subtitle: A warming antiviral decoction for the early days of a cold
excerpt: Dried elderberries and fresh ginger simmered in water to make a concentrated decoction. Taken during the first days of a cold or flu. Elderberries must be cooked, raw elderberries are mildly toxic. The combination is one of the most evidence-supported traditional immune preparations.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), elder entry, elderberry wine and decoction for colds, flu, and fever. WHO Monograph on Sambucus nigra (1999), antiviral and immune-stimulating properties of elderberry extracts. Zakay-Rones et al. (2004), 'Randomized study of the efficacy and safety of oral elderberry extract in the treatment of influenza A and B virus infections', limited clinical evidence for reduced flu duration.

recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the batch and keep refrigerated for up to 5 days. Reheat gently before each dose.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a batch at the first sign of illness. Keeps refrigerated for 5 days, enough for a full course.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation made by simmering plant material (usually roots, bark, or berries) in water for 20, 40 minutes, rather than just steeping. Used for tougher plant parts that require heat to release their constituents.

content.text: Dried elderberries and fresh ginger simmered together in water for 30 minutes, strained, and sipped hot. This is a

rather than an infusion, the berries are too tough and dense for a simple steep, and they must be cooked to destroy the cyanogenic glycosides that make raw elderberries mildly toxic. Maud Grieve notes elderberry decoctions and wines as traditional winter cold and flu remedies; the WHO monograph (1999) documents antiviral properties; and a 2004 randomised trial found that elderberry extract reduced the duration of flu symptoms by an average of four days. The ginger adds warmth, antiemetic action, and an antiplatelet benefit that rounds out the decoction.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Raw elderberries are toxic: raw or undercooked elderberries contain cyanogenic glycosides that cause nausea and vomiting. Always simmer for at least 20 minutes. Do not use fresh elderberries from the garden without thorough cooking; dried elderberries available from herbal suppliers are safe to use in this preparation after simmering.

Pregnancy: elderberry at low culinary doses in cooking is generally considered safe. Therapeutic-dose elderberry decoctions during pregnancy are less studied and best discussed with a midwife.

Anticoagulants: ginger (in combination with elderberry) has mild antiplatelet properties. See the individual ginger safety note for details.

See your doctor for flu symptoms with a high fever lasting more than three days, breathlessness, chest pain, or significant worsening of symptoms after initial improvement. Do not delay seeking medical care by treating flu exclusively with herbal preparations.

items.prepNote: dried Sambucus nigra berries from a herbal supplier
fresh, sliced into thin rounds, about a 3 cm piece
optional, stir in once the decoction has cooled below 60°C

content.text: Place {{dried-elderberries}} of dried elderberries and {{ginger-root}} of sliced fresh ginger in the small saucepan. Add {{water}} of cold water. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, then reduce the heat and simmer covered for 30 minutes. The liquid will reduce and turn very dark purple-red. Strain through a sieve, pressing the berries firmly with a spoon to extract all the liquid. Allow to cool slightly before stirring in honey if using. Drink 100 ml of the warm decoction as a dose; keep the rest refrigerated.

Adults: 100 ml three times daily at the onset of cold or flu symptoms, taken warm. Continue for five to seven days. The traditional course for elderberry preparations is short and targeted, at the early, acute phase of infection. Do not use as a substitute for rest, fluids, and appropriate medical care if symptoms are severe.

Keeps refrigerated in a sealed glass jar for 5 days. Reheat gently in a small saucepan before each dose. Do not microwave, this unevenly heats the decoction and may create hot spots if honey has been added.
When NOT to use this remedy

Never use raw or inadequately cooked elderberries. See your doctor for high fever persisting more than three days, breathlessness, chest pain, confusion, or rapid worsening of symptoms. Do not delay medical care for flu that is causing significant deterioration, antiviral medication is available and effective if started early.
